title: Iran Submits Proposal to Reopen Strait of Hormuz and End War — Nuclear Talks Deferred
detail: Iran submitted a formal proposal to the United States via intermediaries on April 26, 2026 for reopening the Strait of Hormuz and ending the war, according to Axios reporting confirmed by multiple outlets. The proposal deliberately deferred nuclear discussions to a later stage — a significant concession in framing, as the US has consistently listed Iran's nuclear program as a top demand. The move came after President Trump abruptly canceled Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner's planned trip to Islamabad on April 25, leaving Iranian FM Araghchi in Pakistan without an American counterpart. Iran's proposal signaled continued willingness to negotiate on maritime and economic dimensions of the conflict even as the two sides remained far apart on comprehensive terms. Senior Iranian officials cautioned that the proposal should not be interpreted as capitulation — Foreign Ministry Spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei stated Iran has no current plans for further direct negotiation rounds, citing 'growing mistrust' over the US cancellation. The US had not publicly responded to the proposal as of the evening of April 26. The dual-blockade — Iran holding MSC Francesca, Epaminondas, and other seized vessels while the US blockades Iranian ports — continued to freeze commercial shipping.

media.caption: Iran submits proposal to reopen Strait of Hormuz and end war — nuclear discussions deferred

title: Araghchi Meets Sultan Haitham in Muscat — Oman as Iran's Key Diplomatic Bridge
detail: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi traveled to Muscat, Oman on April 26, 2026 and held talks with Sultan Haitham bin Tariq on the ongoing conflict, Strait of Hormuz security, and Iran's diplomatic push for a negotiated settlement. The Muscat leg of Araghchi's tour followed his earlier stop in Islamabad (April 24) and preceded a planned meeting with Russian President Vladimir Putin in Moscow. Oman has historically served as a key back-channel intermediary between Iran and Western powers — most notably during the Obama-era nuclear talks that led to the JCPOA. Araghchi's engagement with Muscat reflects Tehran's continued effort to build a diplomatic coalition and present itself as seeking a negotiated path out of the conflict, even as the US canceled its planned envoy visit and Iran's public posture remained defiant. Omani officials, who maintain cordial relations with both Tehran and Washington, were expected to relay messages about Iran's Hormuz reopening proposal to US interlocutors. Despite the diplomatic activity, Trump continued to insist the US 'holds all the cards' and maintained that Iran must come to the US — not the other way around.

media.caption: Araghchi meets Sultan Haitham in Muscat as Iran conducts intensive diplomatic tour

title: IRGC Claims to Dismantle 84-Person US-Israeli Spy Network Planning Attacks in Western Iran
detail: Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Intelligence Organization announced on April 26, 2026 that it had dismantled a network of 11 operatives and 73 agents allegedly linked to US and Israeli intelligence services, which was plotting military attacks and sabotage operations from Iran's western border regions. The IRGC stated the network had been tasked with conducting attacks against military and civilian infrastructure and gathering intelligence on IRGC units in western Iran's border areas. The announcement was made via Iranian state media and IRGC-affiliated outlets. Independent verification was impossible due to Iran's ongoing internet blackout, which has severely restricted outside journalists' access. The announcement followed previous IRGC claims of disrupted CIA and Mossad operations during the conflict and fits a pattern of the IRGC publicizing counter-intelligence successes as a morale-boosting measure. Western intelligence sources did not comment on the claims. The announcement was also seen as a warning signal that Iran's intelligence services remain active and functional despite 59 days of intensive bombing.

media.caption: IRGC claims to dismantle 84-person US-Israeli spy network plotting attacks in western Iran

title: Bloomberg: Hormuz 'Double Blockade' Halts Ship Traffic for First Time in History — Brent $107.58
detail: Bloomberg reported on April 26, 2026 that the US-Iran dual blockade of the Strait of Hormuz had effectively halted all commercial ship traffic — described as the first time in history that passage through the world's most critical oil chokepoint became virtually impossible from both directions simultaneously. Iran holds the MSC Francesca, Epaminondas, and other seized vessels while the US Navy blockades Iranian-flagged and sanctioned-cargo ships from exiting. Brent crude rose 2.14% to $107.58/bbl; WTI rose 2.08% to $96.36/bbl as oil markets priced in continued disruption. An IRGC spokesperson declared the Strait would 'under no circumstances' return to its previous state even after a ceasefire. UK Prime Minister Starmer called President Trump to discuss the 'urgent need to get shipping moving again,' warning of 'severe consequences on the global economy.' The UN FAO issued a new food price alert citing rising fertilizer and energy costs. European natural gas prices continued their upward trajectory. Approximately 500+ tankers remain stranded in the Gulf region with no resolution in sight.

media.caption: Hormuz double blockade halts ship traffic for first time in history — Brent crude surges to $107.58/bbl
